coredata.dc:title: Multichannel sensor array based on carbon dots and metal ions system for physiological phosphates sensing

coredata.dc:description: The detection of physiological phosphates is crucial for elucidating metabolic processes and related diagnoses of diseases. Herein, a carbon dots (CDs) modulated multichannel fluorescent sensor array was proposed for detecting and distinguishing physiological phosphates. In this sensor, three metal ions (Nd3+, Ag+, and Fe3+) with significantly different binding forces with phosphate ions were selected to trigger fluorescence quenching of three CDs (BCDs, GCDs, and RCDs). Upon adding physiological phosphates, the fluorescence intensity of CDs changed. Depending on the discrepancy in the content of phosphate units in different physiological phosphates, the sensor array realized the detection and differentiation of five physiological phosphates related to adenosine triphosphate (ATP) hydrolysis, and has good anti-interference ability, which can realize the differentiation of physiological phosphates in complex serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Physiological phosphates
Sensor array
Carbon dots
Metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of three kinds of CDs. TEM images of (A) BCDs, (B) GCDs, and (C) RCDs. (D) FTIR, (E) N1s XPS spectra, and (F) O1s XPS spectra of three kinds of CDs. (G-I) The left axis represents the UV–vis absorption spectra of the three kinds of CDs; the right axis represents fluorescence emission spectra of the three kinds of CDs recorded at different excitation wavelengths.

Sensing performance of the sensor array on physiological phosphates (5 μM). (A) Fluorescence response diagram of the sensor array to five physiological phosphates, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of physiological phosphates to the sensor array, respectively. (B) Heat map derived from the fluorescence response for five physiological phosphates. (C) LDA plot, and (D) HCA plot for the discrimination of the five physiological phosphates. The scores were generated through LDA with 95 % confidence ellipses.

(A) LDA plot of the sensor array responses to different concentrations of ATP. (B) Plot of Factor 1 and different concentrations of ATP. Inset: The linear relationship in the concentration of ATP from 0.1 to 10 μM. LDA plots of the sensor array responses to binary physiological phosphates mixtures of (C) ATP and ADP, and (D) ATP, ADP and AMP with different ratios. Total concentration of the mixture was 10 μM. The scores were generated through LDA with 95 % confidence ellipses.

(A) LDA plot of the sensor array identification of five physiological phosphates in the presence of other interfering substances. (B) HCA plot for the discrimination of the physiological phosphates and other interfering substances. The scores were generated through LDA with 95% confidence ellipses.

LDA plots of the sensor array responses obtained against (A) 10 μM of physiological phosphates in the presence of 0.1 % FBS and (B) 50 μM of physiological phosphates in the presence of 1 % FBS. The scores were generated through LDA with 95 % confidence ellipses.

head.ce:title: Multichannel sensor array based on carbon dots and metal ions system for physiological phosphates sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial People’s Hospital, The First-Affiliated Hospital of Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First-Affiliated Hospital of Hunan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Provincial People’s Hospital, The First-Affiliated Hospital of Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of physiological phosphates is crucial for elucidating metabolic processes and related diagnoses of diseases. Herein, a carbon dots (CDs) modulated multichannel fluorescent sensor array was proposed for detecting and distinguishing physiological phosphates. In this sensor, three metal ions (Nd3+, Ag+, and Fe3+) with significantly different binding forces with phosphate ions were selected to trigger fluorescence quenching of three CDs (BCDs, GCDs, and RCDs). Upon adding physiological phosphates, the fluorescence intensity of CDs changed. Depending on the discrepancy in the content of phosphate units in different physiological phosphates, the sensor array realized the detection and differentiation of five physiological phosphates related to adenosine triphosphate (ATP) hydrolysis, and has good anti-interference ability, which can realize the differentiation of physiological phosphates in complex serum samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Physiological phosphates take part in almost all metabolic processes and occupy a special position in life activities. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the primary energy currency within cells, is instrumental in driving a wide array of vital biological activities [1,2]. The ATP metabolic cycle involves its enzymatic hydrolysis to adenosine diphosphate (ADP), adenosine monophosphate (AMP), and phosphate ion (Pi), releasing energy that fuels cellular functions [3,4]. Thus, the various forms of physiological phosphates associated with ATP hydrolysis are crucial for sustaining human life [5]. However, disruptions in ATP hydrolysis can lead to imbalances in physiological phosphate levels, which can derail normal metabolic processes and precipitate a variety of diseases, such as renal failure, hypoglycemia, Parkinson's disease, rickets and so on [6]. Hence, monitoring ATP-related physiological phosphates levels is more than important to prevent the development of disease.

The hydrolysis of ATP involves the presence of a multitude of physiological phosphates. However, the traditional methods including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7,8], electrochemical method [9,10], fluorescence method [11–13], and colorimetry [14,15] are typically capable of identifying a single type of phosphate, which makes them less effective for analyzing mixtures with multiple structurally similar phosphates. Array-based detection method employs multiple cross-reaction sensing units, which vest splendid ability of simultaneously detecting signals from various analytes [16,17]. The use of pattern recognition technology further enhances the array-based method, enabling the classification and quantification of analytes, which is favorable to the detection and identification of multiple phosphates in complex samples [18,19].

Carbon dots (CDs) has recently raised as a promising fluorescent nanomaterial because of its unique chemical structure and optical properties [20,21]. Previous studies showed that the surface of CDs is rich in functional groups, which endowed it excellent optical properties [22–24]. Meanwhile, metal ions could combine with the functional groups on the surface of CDs to form CDs-metal ions complexes, which quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Herein, we proposed a three-channel fluorescent sensor array based on metal ions and CDs mediated for detecting and distinguishing physiological phosphates (ATP, ADP, AMP, Pi, and PPi). Due to Nd3+, Ag+, and Fe3+ have significantly different affinities with phosphate group, the three metal ions were selected separately to trigger fluorescence quenching of three-color-emitting CDs (BCDs, GCDs, and RCDs), among which three CDs had fluorescence emission characteristics of blue, green and red, respectively. When physiological phosphates were added to the CDs-metal ion system, physiological phosphates will compete with CDs to bind metal ions, resulting the fluorescence of the three CDs recovered or further quenched in different degrees. Given the discrepancy in the content of phosphate units in different physiological phosphates bring different molecular structures and metal ions binding functions, the binding affinity between metal ions and different physiological phosphates were distinct, resulting in distinct fluorescence response patterns. Therefore, different physiological phosphates can be detected and distinguished (Scheme S1).

Citric acid, folic acid, and formic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), adenosine 5′-diphosphate sodium salt (ADP), adenosine 5′-monophosphate sodium salt (AMP), sodium phosphate tribasic dodecahydrate (Pi), and sodium pyrophosphate (PPi) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The UV–visible absorption spectrum was measured with a UV-2600i spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectrum was measured with QM40 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PTI, Canada). The fluorescence intensity was recorded on Biotek microplate reader (SpectraMax i3, USA).
Detailed information about the synthesis of BCDs, GCDs, and RCDs are described in the Supporting Information.

In the fluorescence titration experiment, different CDs solution (BCDs 6 µg/mL, GCDs 20 µg/mL, and RCDs 5 µg/mL) was diluted using the NaAc-HAc (10 mM, pH 5.0). Then, different concentrations of metal ions were gradually added to the CDs solution (BCDs, Nd3+, GCDs, Ag+, RCDs, Fe3+). The excitation wavelengths of BCDs, GCDs and RCDs were 370 nm, 470 nm and 560 nm, respectively. Each experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the sensor array and sensing of physiological phosphates
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the three CDs solutions were diluted to the corresponding concentrations (BCDs 6 μg/mL, GCDs 20 μg/mL, RCDs 5 μg/mL). Then the corresponding concentration of metal ions (Nd3+, 40 μM; Ag+, 100 μM; Fe3+, 30 μM) were added. A multichannel fluorescent sensor array was formed by mixing three CDs-metal ions solutions uniformly.

In a 96-well plate, 150 μL CDs-metal ion solution was mixed with 50 μL physiological phosphate solution. Then, the fluorescence intensity of the three emission channels was collected by using an enzyme labeler. The value of (F-F0)/F0 was applied show the fluorescence responses of the sensor array to physiological phosphates, where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of physiological phosphates, respectively. The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of BCDs, GCDs and RCDs are 370/450 nm, 470/550 nm and 560/630 nm, respectively. Each experiment was repeated five times. The data analysis was performed using linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) in Anaconda Prompt.

To evaluate the ability of the sensor array to detect and distinguish various physiological phosphates in complex serum samples, five physiological phosphate solutions containing fetal bovine serum (FBS) and FBS solutions without physiological phosphate were prepared. In a 96-well plate, the blank group, the FBS group and the five physiological phosphates containing FBS were incubated with the sensor array, respectively. Data processing is the same as above.

BCDs, GCDs, and RCDs were synthesized according to previous reports and used to construct the sensor array [25,26]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that the three CDs were uniform sphericity with particle sizes of 2.7 ± 0.4 nm, 3.3 ± 0.4 nm, and 4.0 ± 0.5 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A–C). To investigate the surface functional group properties of the three CDs, Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results revealed that all the three CDs contain rich functional groups of oxygen and nitrogen, while the content had obviously different (Fig. 1D–F and Fig. S1), which leaded to different fluorescence quenching properties of metal ions on the three CDs.

The optical properties of three kinds of CDs were investigated by UV–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. The UV–visible absorption spectra of BCDs exhibited a strong absorption peak at 235 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. The absorption peak at 280 nm and 335 nm is assigned to the n-π* electron transition of the C = O and C = N bonds. Similarly, the GCDs exhibited a much broader absorption bands, with peaks at 340, 420, and 554 nm, while the RCDs have a main absorption band at 556 nm. The results indicated that all the three CDs showed significant π-π* and n-π* transition absorption peaks [25,26]. The fluorescence spectra of three CDs revealed that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were appeared at 370 nm/450 nm, 470 nm/540 nm and 560 nm/630 nm, respectively (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the fluorescence emission spectral properties of the three kinds of CDs at different excitation wavelengths were shown in Fig. 1G–I, which indicated that they had independent emission channels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and construction of the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: To construct the multichannel sensor array for detecting physiological phosphates, three CDs with the emission spectral properties of blue, green, and red were selected as fluorescence sensing units. The three CDs produced little spectral interference with each other, which is conducive to the formation of three independent sensing channels. It has been reported that the metal ions can selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs [27]. Considering the characteristic that metal ions can trigger the fluorescence of CDs quenching, as well as the fact that physiological phosphates have a high affinity for metal ions, three metal ions (Nd3+, Ag+, and Fe3+) with significantly different affinities with phosphates were selected to construct the sensor array.

For improving the sensitivity of the sensor array for detecting physiological phosphates, the concentrations of metal ions were optimized. Fig. S3A–C showed that the fluorescence intensity of three CDs was gradually quenched with the increase of the concentration of Nd3+, Ag+, and Fe3+, indicating that the metal ions can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, correspondingly. The quench of the fluorescence intensity of BCDs can be ascribed to electron or energy transfer and ground-state complex formations between Nb3+ and BCDs [28,29]. The fluorescence quenching of GCDs by Ag+ could be attributed to its chelation with the Ag+, facilitating charge transfer from the excited state of the GCDs to Ag+ [30]. The RCDs fluorescence could be quenched by Fe3+ via static quenching [31]. The fluorescence quenching rates of the three CDs decreased at 40 μM Nd3+, 100 μM Ag+, and 30 μM Fe3+, respectively (Fig. S3D). To obtain the optimal sensing performance, these concentrations were chosen to build sensor array. Notably, the fluorescence of the three CDs can only be significantly quenched by their own corresponding metal ions, even when in contact with other metal ions, having no significant effect on their fluorescence intensity (Fig. S4). In addition, the binding constants (Ka) between the three CDs and the three metal ions were 5.37 × 103 M−1, 1.14 × 104 M−1, and 9.52 × 105 M−1, respectively (Fig. S5 and Table S1). These results indicated that the CDs-metal ions system could provide the characteristics of different affinities, and would achieve different fluorescence responses for each CDs upon adding various analytes.

With the CDs-metal ions systems, the fluorescence response of the three kinds of CDs occurred in different degrees upon the addition of physiological phosphates (Figs. S6-S8). This indicated that the physiological phosphates can competitively binds with metal ions the CDs-metal ions systems. The interaction between metal ions and CDs would be weakened by the competitive coordination of phosphate anions, and thus the metal ions are replaced from the CDs with the addition of phosphates [32,33]. Then, the fluorescence intensity of CDs can be recovered. Because of the discrepancy in the content of phosphate units in different physiological phosphates as well as the different molecular structures, the interactions between each metal ion and physiological phosphate could be recognized. On the contrary, when the systems did not involve metal ions, none of the three CDs showed a significant response to various physiological phosphates (Fig. S9). Therefore, the proposed strategy based on CDs-metal ions system could be applied to develop a cross-reactive sensor array for recognizing of physiological phosphates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: Next, a multichannel sensor array based on BCDs-Nd3+, GCDs-Ag+, and RCDs-Fe3+ were constructed. To appraise the sensing performance of the sensor array, five physiological phosphates were employed as analytes. As shown in Fig. 2 A, there are characteristic fluorescence responses among the five physiological phosphates, suggesting differential interactions of the CDs-metal ion pairs with the physiological phosphates. The result indicated the unique response mode for the sensor array to each physiological phosphate. Heat map also showed a differential fingerprint for the five physiological phosphates (Fig. 2B). Then, the fluorescence responses were analyzed through a supervised pattern recognition algorithm LDA [34,35]. As a result, the five physiological phosphates occupy different positions and can be clearly distinguished from each other. The scatter points represented the responses of the sensor array to physiological phosphates, with every five replicates circled by a 95 % confidence ellipse. The result indicated that the successful differentiation of five physiological phosphates (Fig. 2C). Moreover, HCA was also employed to analyze the response data matrix. Five well-separated clusters correspond to each kind of physiological phosphates were observed, suggesting clear differentiation of the sensor array (Fig. 2D). Meanwhile, the accuracy of classification was evaluated through Jackknifed confusion matrix, certifying 100 % classification accuracy for five physiological phosphates (Table S2). These results validated the feasibility of the sensor array for physiological phosphate detection and recognition. Further, the sensor array showed excellent recognition of physiological phosphates at other concentrations (1 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 50 μM, and 100 μM) (Fig. S10, S11). The 100 % identification accuracy of 40 physiological phosphates unknown samples still more verifies the excellent detection performance (Fig. S12, Fig. S13 and Table S3).

To clarify the quantitative detection capability of the constructed sensor array, ATP was selected as the analyte owing to its pivotal role in physiology. When different concentrations of ATP were added, the fluorescence intensity showed an enhanced trend with the increase of ATP concentration (Fig. S14). LDA diagram displayed different concentrations of ATP occupied different positions in the LDA diagram, which could be clearly distinguished (Fig. 3 A). In addition, different concentrations of ATP were correlated with factor 1, and showed a good linear relationship at the concentration of ATP from 0.1 μM to 10 μM (Fig. 3B).

To verify the capacity of the sensor array for identifying a plurality of physiological phosphates in a mixture, three binary physiological phosphates mixtures of ATP and ADP, ADP and AMP, ATP and AMP were constructed. As shown in the Fig. 3C, Fig. S15 and Fig. S16, the sensor array has excellent ability to distinguish the mixed physiological phosphates, in which it realized the distinction of 11 proportions of phosphate mixed samples (10:0, 9:1, 8:2, 7:3, 6:4, 5:5, 4:6, 3:7, 2:8, 1:9, 0:10). It could be seen from the LDA diagram that the two pure physiological phosphates samples were distributed on both sides respectively, and the mixture of physiological phosphates were distributed in the middle of the two pure samples, and gradually approached the pure physiological phosphates samples with the increase of the proportion. In addition, the sensor array had successfully achieved the distinction between different ratios of ATP, ADP and AMP ternary physiological phosphates mixtures (Fig. 3D). These results demonstrated the favorable feasibility of sensor array to complex physiological phosphates systems.

As to investigate the anti-interference ability of the sensor array, common ions (Ca2+, K+, Na+, Cl−, SO4 2−, Ac−, 50 μM), small molecular (glucose, ascorbic acid, urea, 10 μM), and amino acids (glycine, tryptophan, proline, aspartic acid, 10 μM) were added into the sensor array. LDA diagram showed that the distribution of interfering substances is chaotic and cross seriously, whereas five physiological phosphates were divided into five clusters and were located far away from the interfering substances (Fig. 4 A and Fig. S17). In addition, it was found by HCA that the five phosphates were clustered into five categories, without crossing each other, and could be clearly distinguished from various interfering substances (Fig. 4B). These results extruded the strong anti-interference performance of the sensor array, which laid a foundation for further applications.

For further explore the practicability of sensor array, the ability of the sensor array to recognize physiological phosphates in FBS was investigated. FBS simulates the real physiological environment, in which abundant proteins, electrolytes, and various ions. As shown in the Fig. 5 A, the sensor array can clearly distinguish between the five physiological phosphate samples and the FBS samples. At the same time, at higher concentrations of FBS (1 %), the sensor array also showed a clear response to FBS samples (Fig. S18), and the five physiological phosphates were still distinguished from FBS (Fig. 5B). Moreover, to verify the accuracy of our sensor array, the physiological phosphate content of the FBS samples was also to test using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The results obtained from the two methods were generally in accordance with each other (Table S4). These results further validate the powerful performance of our sensor array to recognize physiological phosphates in complex physiological environments, and are expected to achieve clinical application.

In conclusion, a three-channel sensor array based on CDs-metal ions was proposed for the detection and differentiation of physiological phosphates. The sensor array not only achieved accurate detection and differentiation of five physiological phosphates (ATP, ADP, AMP, Pi, PPi), but also showed excellent detection performance in quantitative detection of ATP. In addition, the sensor array had powerful anti-interference ability, excessive detection sensitivity, and excellent sensing performance in real samples. The establishment of the sensor platform is expected to realize sensitive detection of complex biological samples in clinical applications.

Li Huang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Ting Huang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Junyu Liao: Software, Data curation. Meiling Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Youyu Zhang: Validation, Supervision. Xiaohua Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shouzhuo Yao: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111992.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
